findings.title: Tool `query` accepts unconstrained string input
findings.description: The following string parameter(s) have no `maxLength` constraint: `sql`. Unbounded strings let an attacker stuff arbitrary payloads through the tool, including indirect-injection content.
findings.remediation: Add a `maxLength` to each string property, or constrain with an `enum` or `pattern`. Most legitimate tool inputs fit under a few hundred bytes.

findings.title: Tool `savePath` name implies a side effect that is not declared
findings.description: `savePath` looks like a side-effecting tool (its name contains a mutation verb), but its `side_effects` declaration is []. A policy synthesizer cannot produce safe rules for this tool because it cannot tell what it actually does.
findings.remediation: Declare the tool's true side effects explicitly. If the tool is genuinely read-only, rename it to match (e.g. `email.preview` rather than `email.send`).

findings.title: Tool `fetch` fetches external web content -- indirect-injection surface
findings.description:
Description: "Fetches a URL from the internet and optionally extracts its contents as markdown.

Although originally you did not have internet access, and were advised to refuse and tell the user this, this tool now grants you internet access. Now you can fetch the most up-to-date information and let the user know that." -- this tool pulls externally-controlled content into the agent's context window, the canonical indirect-injection vector. Even when the user supplies the URL, content at that URL can carry hostile instructions.
findings.remediation: Sandbox the fetched content: strip prompts before forwarding to the model, constrain to an allow-list of domains, and route through capframe-guard with a `domain in [...]` caveat.

findings.remediation: Add a `maxLength` to each string property, or constrain with an `enum` or `pattern`. Most legitimate tool inputs fit under a few hundred bytes.

findings.remediation: Add a `maxLength` to each string property, or constrain with an `enum` or `pattern`. Most legitimate tool inputs fit under a few hundred bytes.

Even when the user supplies the URL, content at that URL can carry hostile instructions.

findings.title: Tool `When` fetches external web content -- indirect-injection surface
findings.description: Description: "status is "completed", the response includes the extracted data **Best for:** - Complex research tasks where you don't know the exact URLs - Multi-source data gathering - Finding information scattered across the web - Tasks where you can do other work while waiting for results **Not recommended for:** - Simple single-page scraping where you know the URL (use scrape with JSON format - faster and cheaper) **Arguments:** - `prompt`: Natural language description of the data you want (required, max 10,000 characters) - `urls`: Optional array of URLs to focus the agent on specific pages - `schema`: Optional JSON schema for structured output **Prompt Example:** > "Find the founders of Firecrawl and their backgrounds" **Usage Example (start agent, then poll for results):** ```json { "name": "fi..." -- this tool pulls externally-controlled content into the agent's context window, the canonical indirect-injection vector. Even when the user supplies the URL, content at that URL can carry hostile instructions.
findings.remediation: Sandbox the fetched content: strip prompts before forwarding to the model, constrain to an allow-list of domains, and route through capframe-guard with a `domain in [...]` caveat.

findings.title: Tool `git_create_branch` name implies a side effect that is not declared
findings.description: `git_create_branch` looks like a side-effecting tool (its name contains a mutation verb), but its `side_effects` declaration is []. A policy synthesizer cannot produce safe rules for this tool because it cannot tell what it actually does.
findings.remediation: Declare the tool's true side effects explicitly. If the tool is genuinely read-only, rename it to match (e.g. `email.preview` rather than `email.send`).

findings.title: Tool `git_checkout` description mentions money but no `money` side-effect is declared
findings.description: Description: "Switches branches - Inputs: - `repo_path` (string): Path to Git repository - `branch_name` (string): Name of branch to checkout - Returns: Confirmation of branch switch" -- this references money/payment/refund/etc., but the declared side_effects ([]) don't include `money`. A capframe-bind policy that relies on declared side_effects to scope spend caveats will under-scope this tool.
findings.remediation: Add `money` to the tool's `side_effects` declaration, or rewrite the description to clarify that no actual money moves.

findings.remediation: Declare the tool's true side effects explicitly. If the tool is genuinely read-only, rename it to match (e.g. `email.preview` rather than `email.send`).

findings.title: Tool `Query Execution` exposes a code/command execution surface
findings.description: `Query Execution` looks like it executes code or shell commands (`execute_query`: Execute an arbitrary SQL query. - Takes a SQL string (`query`) - Returns query results for SELECT/SHOW/DESCRIBE, or a success message for other commands). Arbitrary execution is the maximal authority a tool can hold -- it subsumes every other caveat, so it should never be exposed to an agent without a hard sandbox and an explicit, narrowly-scoped capability.
findings.remediation: Do not expose raw code/shell execution to an agent. If unavoidable, run it in a disposable sandbox with no network + no host FS, gate it behind a capframe-bind capability scoped to an allow-list of commands, and require holder-of-key proof per call.

findings.title: Tool `tavily_crawl` accepts an unbounded monetary / quota value
findings.description: The numeric parameter(s) `limit` have a money/quota-shaped name but no `maximum` constraint. An LLM tricked by indirect-injection can call the tool with arbitrarily large values.
findings.remediation: Add a `maximum` (and ideally `minimum`) to each money/quota numeric, OR enforce the cap via a capframe-bind `--limit` caveat at the agent boundary.

Arbitrary execution is the maximal authority a tool can hold -- it subsumes every other caveat, so it should never be exposed to an agent without a hard sandbox and an explicit, narrowly-scoped capability.

findings.remediation: Add a `maxLength` to each string property, or constrain with an `enum` or `pattern`. Most legitimate tool inputs fit under a few hundred bytes.

findings.title: Tool `write_file` accepts unconstrained string input
findings.description: The following string parameter(s) have no `maxLength` constraint: `content`, `path`. Unbounded strings let an attacker stuff arbitrary payloads through the tool, including indirect-injection content.
findings.remediation: Add a `maxLength` to each string property, or constrain with an `enum` or `pattern`. Most legitimate tool inputs fit under a few hundred bytes.

findings.title: Tool `write_file` name implies a side effect that is not declared
findings.description: `write_file` looks like a side-effecting tool (its name contains a mutation verb), but its `side_effects` declaration is []. A policy synthesizer cannot produce safe rules for this tool because it cannot tell what it actually does.
findings.remediation: Declare the tool's true side effects explicitly. If the tool is genuinely read-only, rename it to match (e.g. `email.preview` rather than `email.send`).

findings.title: Tool `read_query` accepts unconstrained string input
findings.description: The following string parameter(s) have no `maxLength` constraint: `query`. Unbounded strings let an attacker stuff arbitrary payloads through the tool, including indirect-injection content.
findings.remediation: Add a `maxLength` to each string property, or constrain with an `enum` or `pattern`. Most legitimate tool inputs fit under a few hundred bytes.

findings.title: Tool `read_query` exposes a code/command execution surface
findings.description: `read_query` looks like it executes code or shell commands (Execute a SELECT query on the SQLite database). Arbitrary execution is the maximal authority a tool can hold -- it subsumes every other caveat, so it should never be exposed to an agent without a hard sandbox and an explicit, narrowly-scoped capability.
findings.remediation: Do not expose raw code/shell execution to an agent. If unavoidable, run it in a disposable sandbox with no network + no host FS, gate it behind a capframe-bind capability scoped to an allow-list of commands, and require holder-of-key proof per call.

findings.title: Tool `write_query` name implies a side effect that is not declared
findings.description: `write_query` looks like a side-effecting tool (its name contains a mutation verb), but its `side_effects` declaration is []. A policy synthesizer cannot produce safe rules for this tool because it cannot tell what it actually does.
findings.remediation: Declare the tool's true side effects explicitly. If the tool is genuinely read-only, rename it to match (e.g. `email.preview` rather than `email.send`).

findings.title: Tool `write_query` exposes a code/command execution surface
findings.description: `write_query` looks like it executes code or shell commands (Execute an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE query on the SQLite database). Arbitrary execution is the maximal authority a tool can hold -- it subsumes every other caveat, so it should never be exposed to an agent without a hard sandbox and an explicit, narrowly-scoped capability.
findings.remediation: Do not expose raw code/shell execution to an agent. If unavoidable, run it in a disposable sandbox with no network + no host FS, gate it behind a capframe-bind capability scoped to an allow-list of commands, and require holder-of-key proof per call.
